trajectory_summary: The call opens on a neutral customer who briefly becomes genuinely engaged when she volunteers her grid-resilience motivation at 01:46 — the call's only energy peak. Holland doesn't anchor on it, pivots immediately to the program pitch, and the call settles into a bill-collection sequence that stalls when Stephanie needs her computer. The call ends suspended mid-task with no appointment booked, no locked callback, and the customer pulled away by another call. The trajectory is stagnant after the 01:46 peak: energy was available and unused.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, no appointment was booked and no locked callback was established. The call ends with Stephanie stepping away to take another call while Holland is waiting on hold for a bill that hasn't arrived. Correct outcome classification: incomplete / no-book. The lead is warm enough to recover on a callback, but nothing was locked.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Interest anchor depth — emotional motivation unused
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Well, just just to say the world.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Stephanie volunteers the call's only genuine emotional signal (grid resilience, fear of power loss) and Holland acknowledges it in two words before moving to the program pitch. Per verify.interest_in_solar, this is the emotional anchor the entire pitch should build from — and per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture without flip is the universal floor failure. On this call the motivation isn't even bill-pain; it's resilience anxiety, which is a stronger and more specific hook. Holland had the material and didn't use it. This is the coaching beat that changes the shape of every future call for this rep.

key_moments.quote: Hey. So this is Holland with Ion. I got a request to remove the solar for your home. How are you today?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland's intro omits two elements the standard intro requires: a clear company introduction that establishes legitimacy, and a reference to what prompted the call (the form submission or lead source). 'I got a request to remove the solar for your home' is garbled — almost certainly 'review' not 'remove,' but the phrasing is unclear enough that a skeptical customer could hear it as odd. No legitimacy anchor is laid. The customer answers cooperatively, so no immediate damage, but the intro does no trust-building work.

key_moments.quote: Well, just just to say the world.
key_moments.stewart_read: Stephanie's motivation is environmental/resilience-driven, not bill-pain-driven. She then immediately expands it at 01:46 into a specific grid-resilience scenario. This is a genuine emotional anchor — she has a concrete fear (grid failure) and a concrete desired outcome (backup power). Holland's response is 'Exactly. K.' — a two-word acknowledgment that closes the thread. Per verify.interest_in_solar, top setters spend 60+ seconds here; Holland spends roughly 10 seconds before pivoting to the battery follow-up and then immediately to the program pitch.

key_moments.quote: Are you interested in, like like, backup batteries and whatnot, it sounds like, case of emergency fees?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland identifies the battery interest signal and asks a follow-up — correct instinct. But the execution has two gaps: (1) the question is vague ('backup batteries and whatnot') rather than naming the Tesla Powerwall specifically, which is the only battery brand Ion carries and a legitimacy-building detail on a Tesla-sourced or Tesla-adjacent lead; (2) the confirmation ('Yeah') is not tied to the appointment — Holland doesn't say 'that's exactly what the specialist will walk you through' or equivalent. The signal is identified but not routed into appointment value.

Stephanie doesn't know her roof age. Holland accepts this with 'Don't know? K. That's fine. Would you say it's still in good condition?' and moves on after Stephanie says 'decent.' Per verify.roof_type_age, roof age over 15 years is a hard DQ condition — and a roof under 2 years of ownership with unknown age is unresolved. Holland's follow-up ('decent condition') is a reasonable workaround for the call, but the qualification gap is real: the closer will walk into an appointment where roof age is unknown and the hard-DQ condition is unresolvable from the transcript.
